items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: What temperature range is the food 'danger zone'?
items.correct: 5 to 63 degrees Celsius - where most bacteria multiply rapidly.
items.why: Students assume danger = very hot. It's the MIDDLE range that's dangerous because bacteria thrive there. Fridges stay below 5; cooking exceeds 63 (often 75+ for safety). The middle is where they multiply.

items.q: Are food miles the only thing that decides a food's environmental impact?
items.correct: No - how the food is produced (heated greenhouses, fertilisers, methane from livestock) often matters more than transport distance.
items.misconception: Yes - the further food travels, the worse it always is for the environment.
items.random_wrong: Food transported by bicycle has a large footprint.
items.why: Food miles are easy to picture, so students over-weight them. Transport is typically a small share of a food's total emissions; production dominates. Lamb shipped from New Zealand can have a lower footprint than some locally produced equivalents.

items.q: Is Fairtrade simply a charity donation added to the price?
items.correct: No - Fairtrade is a trading standard: producers receive a guaranteed minimum price and a premium invested in their communities.
items.misconception: Yes - Fairtrade just means a charity gets some of the money.
items.random_wrong: Fairtrade means the food is organic.
items.why: Students blur ethical labels into 'charity'. Fairtrade restructures the trade deal itself - a price floor protects farmers when markets crash, and the social premium is spent democratically on projects like schools and wells. It is also separate from organic certification.

items.q: How many portions of fruit and veg a day are recommended?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 5. “10” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Eatwell Guide is from which country?
items.why: Correct answer: UK. “USA” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Recommended portions of oily fish per week?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 portion. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
